summary: DHH discusses Danish 3G provider 3's growing pains as it reaches mainstream adoption. After a surge in customers, the company faced scrutiny on national television mixing legitimate complaints (spotty coverage, billing errors) with uninformed consumer grievances. He argues that unmet expectations are the real problem, and that 3 should better communicate the early-adopter nature of 3G technology. He draws parallels to GSM's rocky rollout and suggests customers unwilling to tolerate growing pains should stick with 2G.
key_points: Mainstream adoption makes every problem visible and media-worthy, creating a mixed blessing for new technology companies
Media coverage mixed legitimate issues (coverage, billing) with uninformed complaints, adopting a 'new tech is only bad tech' tone
Unmet expectations are the worst customer confidence killer — 3 should have better communicated the early-adopter tradeoffs
3G technology works well enough for those willing to trade some stability for superior connection speeds
3 was the only Danish player actually delivering 3G despite two other companies holding active licenses
money_quotes: Unmet expectations are the worst customer confidence killer.
If you approach a 3G phone as an early adopter, you're actually likely to be pleasantly surprised.
I'm willing to trade a bit of stability for a killer connection.
New technology does indeed have its share of problems.
3 now has enough customers to make every problem instantly visible and of interest to the mass media. That's indeed a mixed blessing.
key_insight: New technology companies reaching mainstream adoption must proactively manage expectations, because the gap between consumer assumptions and early-adopter reality is what destroys confidence — not the technical imperfections themselves.
